desc:
The large river flows westward, this particular part of it being a home to a
wide collection of water plants that grow within. Some tracks can be seen along
the banks, showing that many animals come here to feed. The grass is fed by the
river, and is lush and green, yet everything around it is barren and dry. A
small landing can be spotted some distance to the east.
name: Wide River

desc:
A wide river flows here, lying in between the endless plains of brown, dry
grass. Infested with water plants, it attracts many animals. The river gently
streams from the mountains to the east. The riverbed is scattered with flat
stones and small rocks, while the riverside is covered with lilies and patches
of green grass. The chirps of nearby birds can sometimes be heard.
name: Large River

desc:
The river flows gently, and the current is not too strong here. The water is
deep, making it impossible to wade through. It is inhabited by small water
animals and fish, who feed either on each other or on the many plants in the
water. The river, coming from the east, continues to the west. At the sides of
the river some patches of lush green grass grow, although further away, the
grass becomes brown and dry.
name: Large River

desc:
The large river that streamed from the east gently approaches a small tributary
in the west. Another river can be seen to the south, soon to join with this one.
The current is not strong, yet it is too deep and wide to allow wading through
the river. The plains surrounding the rivers, nourished by the water, are
greener than elsewhere. The sounds of rapids can be heard from the west.
name: Widening River

desc:
A small creek flowing from the south joins a larger river flowing from the east,
creating a wider waterway that continues westward. The rapidly streaming white
water is strewn with large rocks. The force of the river has washed away a great
deal of earth, undermining the riverbanks considerably. Sloping fields stretch
out in all directions.
name: Joining of Two Rivers

desc:
The stream has become extremely fast and violent, due to the joining of the two
rivers. The water hurls around the rocks sticking out of the riverbed, creating
white coats of foam. The current is very strong and the noise of the water makes
it impossible to hear anything else. The river flows to the west, and to the
east lies a delta where the two smaller rivers joined.
name: Foaming Rapids

desc:
A little slower than the rapids to the east, this stream is still a force to be
reckoned with. It is both deep and wide, making it impossible to wade. Endless
plains of dry grass appear at the sides of the stream. Large rocks lie in the
water, preventing safe passage with a vessel.
name: Wide Stream

desc:
The river, flowing rapidly, makes a brusque turn here, continuing to the north.
Swirled away by the violent stream, it is hard to avoid being smacked against
the sharp rocks protruding from the surface. The violent waves smash against the
cliff wall that looms to the south. Seemingly endless plains lie to the west,
some of them nourished by the river.
name: Fast Flowing River

desc:
Well-nourished by the stream which flows all around save to the south, these
plains have become quite lush and fertile. Thousands of flowers can be seen all
over, the buzzing of nearby insects never allowing this place to be completely
quiet. The plains continue to the south, the influence of water diminishing in
the increasing distance.
name: Well-nourished Plains

desc:
Sloping grasslands stretch out as far as the eye can see, sparsely populated by
small thickets of bushy vegetation. Some isolated trees grow here and there, but
most of the land is covered only by small patches of brown, dry grass. Several
piles of small rocks are scattered through the area, brought by a river that
once flowed here. The hard ground, a mixture of clay and limestone, makes
ploughing near impossible.
name: Sloping Fields

desc:
This hill stands tallest amongst its peers and is topped by an enormous stone.
The stone stands near the edge of a cliff that plunges down nearly vertically
into a river. Dry grass covers the gentle slope of the hill in other directions
leading to plains to the west and more hills to the east.
name: Barrow on the Hills

desc:
Bowing planks of rotting wood create the decaying skeleton of a small shack. The
shack's roof has all but integrated into the earth; the majority of it is
sinking into the moist ground. A sharp slope connects the ground-laden roof with
its counterpart, stubbornly clinging to the tips of the bowing planks of rotting
wood.
name: Ruined Old Shack

desc:
A slim chasm here is surrounded by slimy, muddy walls, and in spots, rivulets of
brown water streak down into the depths below. The water droplets carry the
brown sediment along with them, no doubt further eroding the sides of this
unstable hole.
name: Dripping Hole

desc:
Small pools of fetid, dark water have collected here on the soggy ground, fed by
a constant stream of dripping water from above. Footprints of all sizes dot the
squishy surface, and in the western corner of the cave, a wide hole delves
deeper into the recesses of the ground.
name: Soggy Bend

desc:
Large pools of dark, sediment-laden water have collected in the many wide
depressions here. Thin streams of water constantly break free from the dirt
ceiling high above, causing a constant sloshing in the roiling pools below. The
cave opens to the north, and a hole in the ceiling on the western wall provides
access to a dark continuation of this cave.
name: Sloshy Cave

desc:
Many rocks lie scattered on the floor here, and a few rotten shards of wood poke
out of the sediment. Here and there odd fragments of metal glint amid the
stones. To the north, an old iron gate is visible. The walls are made of smooth
rock, and a wide crack snakes its way from the ceiling to the floor. Strong
wooden beams have been set here to support the roof against collapse.
name: Underground Tunnel

desc:
This coffin-shaped tunnel leads steadily to the north and south, whilst a side
passage leads to the west. Rusted remnants of two parallel iron rails travel
along the tunnel, attesting to its former status as a part of a mine. The rails
end in the south, where some time in the past, a door was manufactured to keep a
mine tightly closed. A pool of water has accumulated on the floor, water
dripping slowly into it from the ceiling.
name: Underground Tunnel

desc:
Though this chamber is fairly small, it is nevertheless too large for its roof
to stay aloft unsupported. For this reason the miners left eight large pillars
of the native sandstone equally spaced through the cavern. Each wall is scarred
by the marks of pick and hammer, but the natural beauty of the rock strata, each
a slightly different shade than its neighbours, still stands out for those who
care to look. The only way out of this cave appears to be through the flooded
tunnel to the east; if anyone had been here when the tunnel flooded, they would
have been forced to swim out, or remain here and perish.
name: Pillared Chamber

desc:
The hard rock walls here indicate the enourmous effort it must have taken to
carve out this cave. The marks of picks and chisels are everywhere in the walls
and the roof. The stone floor is smooth, providing an easy walking surface. The
tunnel is high enough for a man with a horse to pass through. Short stairs have
been made in the north, where the tunnel ascends quickly. The stairs end in a
hole in the roof, which can be covered with a hatch.
name: Rock-walled Tunnel

desc:
Bulky pillars of sandstone stand in a regular pattern throughout this large,
square chamber, supporting the roof above. To the south a ramp way cuts into the
level floor, descending into a passage beyond. A similar ramp climbs out of the
cavern to the east. Rusty iron rails travel along both ramps, but to the south
they quickly vanish beneath a pool of chilly, stagnant water that floods the
tunnel beyond.
name: Abandoned Mine Workings

desc:
This fairly wide tunnel descends to the east and climbs gently to the south,
before levelling off once more. The walls to either side were pockmarked by the
action of hammer and pick, and a deep recess in one wall houses a rickety wooden
ladder that pokes its way through a large hole in the roof to grant access to a
chamber above. Two heavy rails of rusted iron travel along the passageway, and
must once have provided access for the mine cars that would have carried ore
from the workings below.
name: Climbing a Turning Tunnel

desc:
The tunnel opens out here into a massive, rectangular chamber. Vast amounts of
ore must have been removed from this area during the years the mine was in use,
but the roof is held firmly aloft by huge pillars of the native sandstone that
have been left in place in a regular pattern throughout the chamber. Toward one
corner a weighty block and tackle is suspended above a sizeable hole in the
floor. A wooden ladder pokes up through the hole beside it.
name: Abandoned Mine Workings

desc:
An almost coffin-shaped tunnel stretches to the east and south, branching into
two just a short way ahead. The walls to either side are pitted and scored by
the mark of pick and hammer, and sturdy old wooden posts shore up the roof in a
few places. A pair of heavy, rusted iron tracks runs the length of the tunnel,
resting on hefty wooden sleepers. Aged and decaying, all of wood in this tunnel
creaks and groans as if it were struggling to maintain its burden.
name: Creaky Mineshaft

desc:
Where to the north the passage slopes gently down into the earth, here it crests
briefly, creating a level staging area for the mine carts, before making an
almost imperceptible descent towards the end of the tunnel. A lone cart stands
here, its wooden walls rotting away in the damp air. A soft breeze makes the
vegetation at the tunnel mouth dance and shiver. And an occasional stronger gust
of wind rattles by.
name: Windy Mineshaft

desc:
The river, having come from the east, flows to the north through steeply sloping
fields. The current sweeps away a few branches and small logs. The water is not
very deep nor wide, but enough so to prevent wading. The plains surrounding the
river are dank and barren. The western and southern riverbank are too high and
steep to allow climbing. The numerous plants that grow in the river provide food
for little fish and other aquatic creatures, who are occasionally seen swimming
in it.
name: Gentle Stream

desc:
Snaking around amongst the plains, the small river flows from south to west.
East of the creek lies a small, patch of grass. The surroundings consist of
sloping fields which are lush and green due to the abundance of water. The water
is quite clear and appears to be filled with life.
name: Gentle Creek

desc:
Coming from the east, the river slowly flows westwards. The grass surrounding
the river is lush and green, due to the access to water. In the water, numerous
diverse plant species flourish, luring numerous little animals who come for
food. The steep riverbank to the south is high and slippery, making it
impossible to climb southwards.
name: Shallow Waters

desc:
The small river flows from the east to the west. Various plants grow in the
water, forming the diet for the herbivorous fauna of the river. A sea of lush,
green grass lies to the north. A sandy and slightly muddy riverbank lies to the
south.
name: Gently Flowing River

desc:
These fields are mostly covered with bright green grass, due to the irrigation
of the rivers which flow on the north and south. This easy access to water
allows the plant life on these fields to grow more lush than the brown grass and
dried out thorn bushes that seem to be the predominant vegetation on the far
side of the two rivers.
name: Bright Green Fields

desc:
The plains grow lush and green here. Grass predominates, interspersed with
various kinds of flowers and weeds. A small river to the south and a larger one
to the north, both flowing westwards, provide natural irrigation. The lush lands
continue to the west and to the east, but beyond either river lie dry
wastelands.
name: Lush Grassland

desc:
The fields are surrounded by the water of two rivers on three sides. The easy
access to water allows the vegetation, which mostly consists of grass, to grow
lush and green. The green fields continue eastwards, where the rivers provide an
excellent natural irrigation.
name: Sea of Grass

desc:
The small, slowly flowing river finds its way from the east to the north. The
waterway is wide but neither very deep nor very fast, making this an excellent
spot to cross. The water is home to many water plants, feeding various fish,
which in their turn provide a food source to the numerous animals found near the
river. The fields close to the river are covered with small patches of lush,
green grass. The further away from the water, the more dry and brown the area
becomes.
name: Trickling Stream

desc:
The brush here is littered with small stones and several rocks as it continues
to the west. Several small, misshapen trees grow here among the rocky brush
trying to eke out an existence. In the north lies a swampy area, made soggy by a
river flowing in the northeast. Rocky fields can be spotted to the east.
name: Thorny Ridgeline

desc:
The small river flows from the south to the west. The water is not very wide,
but it is too deep to wade through it completely. The vegetation around the
waterway is a great deal greener than the rest, due to the access to water. Seas
of lush, green grass lie to the east. A larger river flows in the north, joining
up with this one further to the west.
name: Small Creek

desc:
The small river that flowed here from the east is about to join up with a much
larger river, which came from the northeast. The current slowly grows stronger
and the fish seem much larger than in the small stream. The plains in the direct
vicinity of the river are covered with lush green grass, but the further away
from the water the grass grows, the drier and the browner it becomes. The sound
of rushing water can be heard, coming from the north.
name: Small Tributary

desc:
A huge, broken boulder lies in the middle of the plains. The ground around it is
covered with smaller fragments of rock and broken stones, making it quite
difficult to traverse. Thick weeds and dry grass grow amongst the rubble. Some
huge piles of debris lie to the west, preventing passage in that direction. The
sloping plains continue to the south and the west, while a small river flows by
to the north.
name: Huge Boulder in the Dry Grass

desc:
A multitude of rocks surround the scarce vegetation of dried out grass and the
occasional thorny bush or isolated tree that has appeared. The rubble blocks the
path in every direction, save for the west and the south. To the west the
brambles and thorn bushes start to populate the land.
name: Stony Fields

desc:
Sloping fields, covered by dark patches of dry grass and thorny bushes grouped
in small thickets, can be found here. The earth beneath seems to consist mostly
of clay. Small rocks scatter the area, and scarce broken stones lie here and
there, making the footing treacherous. A wall of rock along a ridge runs north
to south along the west. There is a crack in the boulder that sits to the west.
name: Thickets along a Ridge

desc:
Low bushes grow expansively here among the rocky ground. The vegetation grows in
a tangled mess among scattered patches of brown, dried-out grass. Broken stones
and small boulders lie scattered across the land, probably brought here by a
river that found a new course long ago.
name: Dry Brush

desc:
The sloping plains, stretching out for miles in all directions, are covered by
small patches of brown, dry grass, scarcely interrupted by thin thickets of
thorny brush or a lone, misshapen tree. Small rocks and broken stones are
scattered across the land. The bushes and thickets grow thicker to the west and
the south.
name: Sloping Fields

desc:
Sloping fields stretch out towards the horizon in all directions. The
predominant form of vegetation found in this region is dark brown, dry grass
that grows in small patches in an irregular pattern. The hard ground seems to be
a mixture of clay and limestone, rendering profitable agriculture all but
impossible.
name: Sloping Plains

desc:
An old tree stands in the middle of the brush, its trunk split halfway in two by
lightning long ago. A few branches, some of them scorched, lie on the ground.
The brush appears empty save for the tree and low, thick thorny bushes. The
thorn bushes grow taller and thicker to the south, such that they block the
passage in that direction.
name: Old Tree in a Field

desc:
Little can grow in this infertile ground. The only vegetation to be seen is
parched, brown grass and the odd thorny bush. To the south and the east these
thorny bushes have prospered despite the arid conditions and have formed a
natural, impenetrable barrier.
name: Slightly Bushy Plains

desc:
A great deal of caution is needed when crossing these plains because they are
covered in thousands of boulders of all different sizes and shapes, some of
which are fractured and others intact. Small patches of brown, dried-out grass
can be spotted between the rocks, the only visible form of vegetation.
name: Rocky Grassland

desc:
The ground under the bushes here is covered with piles of both large and small
stones. The vegetation here is composed of thick patches of brown thorn bushes
and dry grass. The vegetation thins out to the north and east into fields.
Further to the east and south, an ancient broken road can be seen.
name: Stony Briars

desc:
A grid is formed by numerous large honeycombs arranged in meticulous order. They
are large, perfectly hexagonal sheets of honey and wax that are extremely sticky
and impede progress. On the other hand, the bees constantly come and go,
flitting around and forming tight swarms. There are no obvious differences
between the bee pastures outside and inside the hive, but everything gets
louder, from the resonant drone to the absolutely penetrating.
name: Beehive

desc:
The sloping fields stretch out towards the horizon in all directions. The ground
rises into hilltops to the east and south. The area is mostly vegetated by
small, dark clumps of coarse grass. The course grass is interspersed by a number
of dense thickets of dark green, thorny bushes. Small rocks and stones lie
scattered all about, growing far greater in number, further to the north.
name: Endless Fields

desc:
This hilltop is vegetated by some tiny patches of dry, brown grass. The ground
is a hard-packed mixture of red-brown clay and limestone, that leaves any form
of agriculture, quite unprofitable. A road lies to the southeast, curving
throughout the landscape. Several small stones and broken boulders lie scattered
around the land. The barren wastelands stretch out in all directions.
name: Hilltop near a Cliff

desc:
The hard-packed clay has made these lands largely infertile. A few patches of
grass grow here, although they are mostly brown, due to lack of hydration. A
thick, tangled wall of thorn bushes blocks the way north. The steep cliff wall
of the ridge looms to the west, blocking the way. The fields continue to the
east and south.
name: Infertile Fields

desc:
The green grasslands from the north meet the barren waste from the east. The
change from dry grasslands to verdant plains can be seen very clearly here as
the vegetation becomes less lush than it was to the north. A muddy stream lies
to the west, slowly flowing from its source to the southwest.
name: Mixed Grassland

desc:
The plains here are green with vegetation, since the river is not far to the
west. The foliage consists mainly of lush, green grass and several trees,
interspersed with a few thickets of thorny bushes. The channels continue
eastwards, irrigating the plains there as well, making them equally lush.
name: Green Plains at a River

desc:
The plains have become somewhat waterlogged, lying at the bank of the stream to
the west. Most of the grass has sunk in the mud and small pools appear all
around. The scarce vegetation is composed mostly of smaller, water-loving
plants. Algae float in the small pools, making it hard to distinguish them from
the grass.
name: Moist Plains

desc:
The fields here are a sea of grass that seem to be a grazing spot for animals.
The green fields continue to the east, whilst the original barren plains stretch
out to the south. The land becomes significantly more bushy and thorny as it
leads towards the north.
name: Green Grasslands

desc:
Green grass can be found here that has been grazed short by some ungulates
judging from the scat. The sloping land is fairly barren but gets progressively
bushier and hillier to the southeast. More fields can be seen stretching out
across the horizon to the north.
name: Grazed Fields

desc:
The barren hills are covered in tangles of thorny plants and dried-out grass,
occasionally highlighted by a single, lone tree. It is impossible to produce or
engage in any type of agriculture because of how dry and hard the ground is.
Small pebbles and broken stones are strewn over the slopes. To the east, a cliff
formed by big rocks prevents further movement.
name: Barren Hills near a Cliff

desc:
The field is abundant with sun-grown grass and clover. To the south and east are
vast wastelands, and to the north are lusher pastures. A decrepit old cottage
and some ruins can be seen immediately to the south, and a little trail can be
seen heading away from them.
name: Declining Fields

desc:
A dense thicket of thorny bushes somehow clings to life here, in the middle of
the endless, barren wastelands; its roots sticking out of the ground. A dense
tangle of branches, all covered in small, sharp thorns, blocks the way east and
south. Sloping fields lie both to the north and west, while a small trail can be
spotted to the south through the thick foliage.
name: Large Bush in the Fields

desc:
Not even the smallest patch of grass grows on these dusty hills. The ground, a
compact mixture of clay and limestone, is littered with broken rocks and small
stones. The sloping hills continue in all directions, but the path westward is
blocked by a dense thicket of thorny bushes.
name: Dusty Hills

desc:
A large amount of thick bushes, most of them rather thorny, grow here in these
arid conditions. Little other vegetation is to be seen save for dried patches of
brown grass. The bushes obstruct the passage to the north, east and south. Large
boulders form a rock wall to the east and south which only allows passage to the
west from here.
name: Bushy Hill

desc:
Only scant vegetation and brown patches of grass can be found on these hills.
The hills gradually become much drier than they are here as they spread out for
miles to the south. To the west, in the distance, is a little structure. Along
the east side of a ridge, a rock wall stretches north to south. The boulder that
is to the east has a crack in it.
name: Grass-covered Hills near a Ridge

desc:
Shrubs and thorn bushes cover the ground, engulfing many young saplings and
strangling them. The overgrowth is disrupted by a winding path which leads north
and south through the thorny foliage. The west and east are an impenetrable wall
of nasty thorns preventing any movement through the area.
name: Thorny Clumps

desc:
A winding path through the large clumps of thorn bushes runs here from the west
and bends southward, continuing to more brush to the south. The thick thorn
bushes to the north and the east have entangled themselves into an impenetrable
wall of vegetation with nasty, large thorn spikes.
name: Thornbushes

desc:
This area of fields is vegetated by a number of large thickets of thorny brush
that surround a pool of brownish water. Any passage northwards and southwards is
blocked by dense thickets of thorn brush. The only clear passage is to the west
or east down a winding path that runs through the clumps of thorny foliage.
name: Thorny Clumps

desc:
Deep rich green leaves pervade the thick, healthy thorn bushes here. The thorns
that grow on the thorn bushes are long and strong. The thick thorn bushes grow
with enough space for a traveller to move through without getting pricked too
much from the vegetation. A small field lies far to the east, vegetated with
larger thickets of brush. A river lies to the south, curving its way from north
to south. The river flows steadily, causing little noise. The plains surrounding
these lands seem to stretch on for ages, forming endless seas of wasteland with
clumps of brush throughout.
name: Lush Thorn-bushes

desc:
This area is mostly covered with small patches of dry, brown grass, save for the
occasional tree or thorn bush. Several broken rocks scatter the land, covered
with scarce pieces of moss. The faint glimmer of a flowing river can be seen to
the west. The path to the east is blocked by a huge, broken boulder, surrounded
by a thicket of thorny bushes. Every now and then the high-pitched call of a
bird can be heard in the distance.
name: Dry Grasslands

desc:
Neither very fast nor very slow, this small river comes from the south, becoming
more and more wide as it flows westwards. It is well vegetated by different
water plants, which feed various animals. The plains to the east drain the river
and has therefore become quite swampy.
name: Bend in a River

desc:
Flowing gently northwards, the narrow stream is still shallow enough to allow
wading. Some of the water is being drained by the swampy plains that lie to the
northeast and have turned the surrounding plains lush and green. The water is
extremely muddy and is overgrown with water plants.
name: Shallow Stream

desc:
The stream, although still filled with mud, sluggishly flows from south to
north. The stream flows to here from the south, where a small water source
arises to form a large pool of mud. The vegetation here is dry and barren and
only sloping wastelands cover the surroundings.
name: Muddy Stream

desc:
The area is covered by seemingly endless brown and dry grass. The gentle hills
slowly decline to the south. A patch of thorny bushes lies to the southwest. The
grass becomes greener to the north. The hills loom to the north and finally meet
a wall of rock which cannot be passed.
name: Grassy Hill

desc:
The sloping hills come to a halt as two rivers block their path to the north and
the east. The sudden cliff that drops to the river does not allow passage. The
noise of streaming water fills the area. The plains stretch out as far as the
eye can see to the south and west. Several piles of rocks lie scattered around
the clay and limestone land.
name: Corner of the Hills

desc:
For miles in every direction, there are more sloping fields. The barren wastes
occasionally grow a small clump of prickly shrubs or a lone, deformed tree. The
high-pitched chirp of small birds can be heard. Agriculture would be challenging
due to the ground's tough, wet clay and limestone.
name: Endless Plains

desc:
Dry, brown patches of grass cover most of this area. Only infrequently does the
occasional thick shrub or thorny bush interrupt. The sloping fields continue in
all directions save to the north, where a river flows. The shrubs grow denser
and the patches of grass grow greener the closer they are to the river. A bird's
piercing call can occasionally be heard in the distance.
name: Dried-Out Grasslands

desc:
The wide, barren wastelands are covered with dark patches of dry, brown grass.
Small, thorny bushes sprout up every few hundred feet, but no other form of
vegetation can be seen. The fields to the southwest are greener than here, due
to irrigation which was placed long ago.
name: Bush-covered Plains

desc:
These plains are fairly well vegetated by small, thorny bushes, however they are
not dense enough to make travelling too strenuous. The thorn bushes grow denser
to the southwest, blocking passage in that direction. Some grassy hills can be
seen to the north across the plain of dry bushes.
name: Bushy Plains

desc:
Large heaps of hard stone litter the plains, making mounted navigation
impossible. The barren wastes occasionally grow a small clump of prickly shrubs
or a lone, deformed tree. To the west, a thicket of thorn bushes forms an
insurmountable barrier that blocks access.
name: Rocky Plain

desc:
It is challenging to navigate through the rocky fields since they are uneven and
littered with boulders of various sizes. The desolate wastelands are covered in
little patches of dried-out grass, occasionally broken up by a lone tree or a
small thorn shrub. To the west, a thick wall of prickly vegetation has sprouted,
blocking any further movement.
name: Rocky Plain

desc:
This tiny refuge, surrounded by enormous downed logs and covered in soft, fuzzy
green moss, serves as the residence of several forest creatures. Half of the den
is covered by a particularly large fallen tree, creating a shield from the
weather.
name: Burrow

desc:
This tunnel's floor is built of clay and rocks. Crawling creatures move about in
the shadows and between the rocks. At the northern end of the tunnel, the
ceiling and floor are in contact, although they diverge in the direction of the
huge cave to the south. The rocky ceiling has a large, deep fissure in it.
name: Sloping Tunnel

desc:
Here, a sizable natural cavern with a high ceiling and substantial, pure granite
columns, has developed. The cavern is completely dry and looks as though it has
been free of moisture for many years, but the smooth stone implies that water
once ran through it.
name: Natural Cavern

desc:
A dry cavern formed inside the hard granite mountainside has been altered to
serve as a modest abode. The cavern appears to have developed gradually over
time and stretches north to a natural cavern. The exit to the east is obstructed
by a lot of rubble.
name: Modest Abode

desc:
Despite the fact that these caverns were undoubtedly built by nature, the
inhabitants who presently reside there have converted them into simple
habitations. While the exit to the west is covered by debris, the exit to the
south has a strong stench of blood and meat.
name: Crossing in a Damp Cave

desc:
The walls of this wet cave are black on all sides. Green algae make the floor
slippery. The ceiling of the cavern is formed by large stalactites, formed by
the constant dripping of the chalky rocks that have merged over the eons into a
solid wall of folded rock. The cave continues to the east and south.
name: Damp Cavern

desc:
Hidden from the outside world, this cave shows many signs of having served as a
refuge for various animals. The walls, floor, and ceiling of the cave are
drenched with green algae that thrive in the murky water. The northern and
eastern sides of the cave are covered in knee-deep murky water with spots of
algae floating on it. The actual water appears to be surface water that seeps
through the clay bottom. A large boulder covers a part of the ceiling.
name: Damp Cave

desc:
This cave is dark and small. The walls are flat and damp with moisture and
shimmer from the small amount of light that enters from above. The cave has no
corners or edges, and is roughly spherical in shape. The cave opens into a
tunnel to the west. The tunnel is round and cylindrical in shape.
name: Small Cave

desc:
The walls of the tunnel are made of smooth pitch black stone. The shape of the
tunnel is cylindrical, and the walls are damp from some unseen source of water.
The tunnel continues to the west and the very little amount of light which emits
from the cave to the east is gone to the west.
name: Dark Tunnel

desc:
Puddles of murky water cover the entire length of this underground natural
tunnel. Cold, unyielding rock and a thick reddish-brown mud that appears to be
so viscous as to be seeping into the chamber even now make up its walls. Tiny
glimmers and sparkles suggest that the sticky, oozing wall may contain hidden
treasures.
name: Black Tunnel

desc:
Jagged black crystals line the walls in this domed cave. A corrupted shrine sits
in the middle of the round room. The shrine appears to be an altar to a
malevolent and corrupt force. The lower part of the walls has faint inscriptions
of evil runes and depictions of hideous scenes and torture. The burning of
numerous old sacrifices has left stains on the altar room's ceiling.
name: Black Altar

desc:
A large amount of bushes, most of them quite thorny, have grown very close
together. The light armoured or bootless traveller might encounter some problems
to traverse through the sharp thorns unharmed. But a small opening has been
formed here that might provide some shelter from the seemingly ever present
prevailing winds.
name: Opening in the Thick Bushes

desc:
The stable walls are in a state of decay and deteriorating, including the roof
above. Although it is clear the building itself has not been maintained in many
years, the natural earth and dust along the ground appear disturbed, with more
dust, gravel and rocks along the north and south walls. An impression within the
dirt leads from the stable door along the western wall to the eastern wall where
it disppears beyond sight beneath the wooden planks.
name: Decrepit Stable

desc:
The shed appears aged and unsafe, with the roof caved in slightly in several
places. Years of weather and being unmaintained caused the shed to be at a state
of disrepair. The planks are rotten and damaged along the ground from the rain
and snow that have entered over time, with a select few along the western wall
that were poorly replaced. The door of the shed is uneven from the weight and
years on the hinges that are loose upon on the eastern wall.
name: Derelict Shed

desc:
Uneven wooden boards, warped by age and damp, compose the floor of this long,
low-roofed room. A thick carpet of green mould covers much of the floor, as well
as the shattered remains of four heavy oak tables. The last few pieces of
kindling by the fireplace look suspiciously like the legs of a chair. At one end
of the room a long, sturdy counter rests atop a heavy wooden base, but some of
its boards have been pried loose and pressed into service to block the back door
of the structure entirely. Each of the windows has also been boarded over,
though not so thoroughly as to prevent the entry of all natural light.
name: Ruined Inn

desc:
The air here seems heavy and unwholesome. A noisome aroma pervades every corner
and the walls are black with mildew. The most noticeable feature of this place,
however, are the thick strands of sticky silk that crisscross from one wall to
the other, extending from the uneven boards of the roof above to the chilly
flagstones of the floor underfoot. The tiny bones of countless small creatures
lie strewn all about the room.
name: Dank Cellar

desc:
Built amid the roof trusses, the loft offers little head room, except to those
of short stature. Front to back it is as wide as the inn below, with no attempt
having been made to wall off the near useless floor space where the roof slopes
right down to meet the rough floorboards. All around the room, but concentrated
at the angles of the roof, lie the scattered remains of discarded wooden crates
and boxes. Oddly, the loft does not seem nearly so long as the room below.
name: Small Loft

desc:
The tunnel begins here and leads to the east, with six large beams made from a
thick and sturdy wood to support the ceiling, which was also made of thick
wooden panels. A small table, old and dusty, stand in the north-west corner of
the entrance, with some of the chairs broken and in pieces along the ground. The
western wall is made of stone and the floor is lined with flagstones; they
appear to continue beneath the wall as if they continue to something beyond.
name: Entrance to a Tunnel Underpass

desc:
The tunnel comes to an abrupt end here in a tangle of fallen rock and fractured
wooden posts. The roof above is comprised of a single, massive slab of sandstone
that now lies at a severe angle, making it quite low toward the back. Sturdy
iron tracks vanish beneath the mound of boulders, indicating at one point this
was a mine, although it seems it was abandoned for those purposes a long time
ago.
name: Cave-in

desc:
Deep tunnels can be reached by going down a long flight of stairs that have been
cut into the unyielding rock. Any sharp or protruding stones from the walls have
been removed, and the cavern floor has been cleansed of abnormalities. This cave
could almost pass for a hallway with a little decor and lights.
name: Deep Tunnel

desc:
This modest cave has been transformed into a basic dwelling, and the cavern
system ends here. The occupants must be enormous based on the size of the long,
wooden bunks against the back wall. Although the worst of the sharp edges have
been removed, the walls are still rough.
name: Modest Cave

desc:
Large jutting rocks line the floor, ceiling, and walls of this crude cave
entrance. Several sets of animal bones lie in various piles, in differing states
of decay. It is apparent that a large predator of some sort uses this cave as
its home.
name: Wide Cave Entrance

desc:
This cave opens from the east in a wide fashion and begins to narrow as it
continues to the west. The large, jutting rocks that line all surfaces of this
cave make it somewhat difficult to traverse through the cave. Debris lines the
cracks that run between the jutting rocks on the cave floor.
name: Wide Cave

desc:
The eastern side of this cave tunnel begins to open to a wider part of the cave.
The jutting rocks that line all surfaces of the cave begin to smooth as it runs
from the east to the west. Foul smells emanate from the cave below.
name: Narrowing Cave

desc:
The natural earth forms a depression below, though the ground itself is steady
and packed. A constant gust of air flows through the underground cavity here,
presumably entering from high above. The cavern walls that curve around in all
directions are made of large wedges of slabs and pieces of slate, and appear to
be embeded into the earth on purpose to form massive walls. The wedges block any
passage to three openings within the natural earth walls to the west, north and
south, where another underground area must be. The slabs to the east were tilted
to the side, where an opening to another underground area is discerned.
name: Gusty Underground Cavity

desc:
The walls of earthen rock in the tunnel lead from the north and curve towards a
pile of crushed stone, rocks and other debris to the west. Gusts of air are
coming from that direction, leaving various scents throughout the tunnel of
crisp pine and soil. A large slab leans against the tunnel wall to the east,
used to cover a section of the tunnel wall for more support or protection, and a
draft can be felt passing through to another section beyond.
name: Tunnel Crook

desc:
The tunnel curves in a west and south direction, with sharp edges of slate along
the eastern wall. Rocks and pebbles are piled up along the corner to the
north-east of the tunnel crook, leaving the air dusty and dry. Along the worn
ground are more pebbles and small stones scattered randomly, but clearly from
the impression in the earth it was trampled over many times by something.
Whatever it may be, it must have been large or consistent!
name: Tunnel Crook

desc:
The tunnel continues to the west and east from here, which is apparent from the
depressed earth along the ground. Barely discernible to the south is a small
crosscut leading to another tunnel or cavern beyond, through a portion of the
tunnel wall that has collapsed. A heavy slab leans against the tunnel wall to
the north, protecting another portion of the wall that has sustained damage.
name: Tunnel Crosscut

desc:
The tunnel curves in a east and south direction from here, with sharp edges of
slate along the western wall. A large pile of rocks and pebbles consume the
north-west corner of the tunnel crook, layered with dust. Along the worn ground
in the centre are smaller pebbles and stones, and judging from the impressions
upon the earth with the dusting only along the edges of the tunnel, it has been
trampled over recently.
name: Tunnel Crook

desc:
The tunnel continues to the north and south from here, which is apparent from
the depressed earth along the ground. Slightly visible to the east is a small
crosscut leading to another tunnel or cavern beyond, through a portion of the
tunnel wall that has collapsed. A heavy slab leans against the tunnel wall to
the west, protecting another portion of the wall that has sustained damage.
name: Tunnel Crosscut

desc:
The tunnel curves in a north and east direction from here, with sharp edges of
slate along the western wall. Dust has settled along debris of pebbles, rocks,
and jagged pieces of slate along the edges of the depressed and packed earth of
the centre of the tunnel. Spider webs, old and new, suspend along the tunnel
wall, and toward the south-eastern portion.
name: Tunnel Crook

desc:
The tunnel continues to the west and east from here, which is apparent from all
of the depressed earth along the ground. A large portion of the tunnel wall to
the north was damaged, leaving a large opening to a cavern beyond. The pieces of
rock and slate that form the debris appear easy to get through, but the sharper
slate shards are angled facing the north, preventing anything to enter this part
of the tunnel coming back from that direction. A heavy slab leans against the
southern wall, concealing another opening.
name: Tunnel Crosscut

desc:
The cavern is dark and a fine dust lingers in the air. Portions of every wall
appear to be crumbling and starting to collapse, other than to the south. To the
south a slab of large and sharp slate was used to attempt to block the cave off
from the tunnel beyond or to keep something or someone within.
name: Dark Cavern

desc:
The cavern is dark and a fine dust lingers in the air. Portions of every wall
appear to be crumbling and starting to collapse, other than to the east. To the
east a slab of large and sharp slate was used to attempt to block the cave off
from the tunnel beyond or to keep something or someone within.
name: Dark Cavern

desc:
The cavern is dark and a fine dust lingers in the air. Portions of every wall
appear to be crumbling and starting to collapse, other than to the north. To the
north a slab of large and sharp slate was used to attempt to block the cave off
from the tunnel beyond or to keep something or someone within. Suspended
throughout the ceiling and in the corners are large webs.
name: Dark Cavern

desc:
The tunnel appears to end here in an unnatural and penetrating darkness that any
light could barely penetrate besides possibly standing right next to the source
of it. The wall to the north has been heavily damaged, leaving a pile of debris
with wedged pieces of sharp slate and other portions of the tunnel wall in a
pile. Although easy to enter the opening, there are large pieces of slab and
slate with sharp edges blocking passage back here. Thick, heavy and smoldering
to the east is the dark. A very intense dark. So dark in fact, that it simply
cannot be discerned what makes up the dark or what it truly is.
name: Dark Underground Crosscut
